worth_it_explanation: An ads.txt or app-ads.txt file is the public record of who a publisher authorizes to sell its inventory, so the Equativ lines in it decide whether buyers treat Equativ-sold impressions as authentic or as unauthorized supply they should avoid. A wrong seller account ID, a missing DIRECT or RESELLER designation, or a stale line from a relationship that ended can cost fill and price on bids that would otherwise clear. The check reads a file the publisher already publishes, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific line a publisher can correct.
intro: ads.txt and its app-ads.txt counterpart are plain-text files a publisher posts at the root of each domain to declare the companies authorized to sell its inventory, a standard from the IAB Tech Lab Authorized Digital Sellers project. For a publisher monetizing through Equativ, the Equativ lines in that file tell buyers that impressions offered through Equativ are genuine publisher supply. This subcategory is about the accuracy of those Equativ lines, the seller account ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER designation, and about removing lines that no longer belong. These checks confirm that the file authorizes Equativ correctly and carries nothing stale or unauthorized that could mislead a buyer.

audit_checks.title: The ads.txt file lacks a correct Equativ line with the right account ID and seller type
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a publisher sells through Equativ but the ads.txt file carries no Equativ line, or a line with the wrong seller account ID or a missing DIRECT or RESELLER designation, buyers can read the Equativ-sold inventory as unauthorized and decline to bid on it. That suppresses demand on real inventory the publisher meant to sell, so the cost lands directly on fill and revenue rather than on a back-office detail.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is a list, one authorized seller per line, that a publisher posts at the root of its domain so buyers can confirm which companies may sell its inventory. Each line carries three required fields and one optional field. The first field is the advertising system's canonical domain, which for an Equativ seat is the domain Equativ publishes for this purpose. The second is the seller account ID, the identifier Equativ assigns to this specific publisher. The third is the relationship type, either DIRECT when the publisher sells through Equativ directly or RESELLER when another party is authorized to sell the inventory on the publisher's behalf. The optional fourth field is the certification authority ID. The standard for this format comes from the IAB Tech Lab Authorized Digital Sellers project.

A buyer's demand-side platform reads ads.txt to decide whether a bid request is authentic. When a request claims to come from a publisher but the publisher's ads.txt does not list the seller account the request rode in on, the buyer can treat the impression as unauthorized and decline to bid. So a missing Equativ line, a line with the wrong account ID, or a line that says DIRECT where the relationship is RESELLER can all lead buyers to skip impressions the publisher genuinely meant to sell.

For apps, the same record lives in an app-ads.txt file at the developer's domain rather than the app store listing. A publisher monetizing both web and app inventory through Equativ needs the correct Equativ line in each file since buyers check the file that matches the inventory type.

A few signs point to a missing or incorrect Equativ authorization:
  • The ads.txt file at the publisher's root domain contains no line for the Equativ advertising-system domain
  • An Equativ line exists but carries a seller account ID that does not match the ID Equativ assigned to this publisher
  • The relationship field reads DIRECT for inventory Equativ sells as a reseller, or RESELLER for inventory the publisher sells directly
  • Web inventory has an ads.txt line but app inventory has no matching app-ads.txt line, or the reverse
You may want to confirm the Equativ line matches the account details Equativ provides for this publisher and that the relationship type reflects how the inventory is actually sold. One approach is to compare the line in the live file against the seller account ID and designation in the Equativ account record.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media site often sells the same inventory through more than one path, for example directly to Equativ on some units and through a partner that resells on others. You may want to check that each Equativ line carries the relationship type, DIRECT or RESELLER, that matches how that specific inventory is sold since a single wrong designation can put a slice of demand at risk.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content site that runs ads alongside its catalog may have added Equativ after the storefront was already live, so the ads.txt file can predate the monetization setup. You may want to confirm the Equativ line was actually added when monetization began rather than assumed since a file that was never updated will carry no Equativ authorization at all.

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration documentation for the seller account ID and ads.txt line a publisher should use is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Read the live ads.txt and app-ads.txt files, find the Equativ lines, and compare each line's seller account ID and relationship type against the account details Equativ provides for this publisher.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the publisher's root domain followed by '/ads.txt' (e.g., example.com/ads.txt) and read the plain-text file.
Find the lines whose first field is the Equativ advertising-system domain and record the seller account ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER designation on each.
If the publisher monetizes app inventory, open the developer domain followed by '/app-ads.txt' and repeat the same read for the Equativ lines there.

Compare the recorded account ID and relationship type against the seller account record Equativ provides for this publisher, and note any line that is missing, carries a mismatched ID, or uses the wrong designation.

ads_txt_validator.label: Public ads.txt validator
steps: Run the publisher's domain through a public ads.txt validator to confirm the file parses and to surface malformed lines.
Check that the Equativ line appears in the parsed output with all three required fields present.
Record any syntax error the validator flags on the Equativ line since a malformed line is read the same as a missing one by buyers.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each inventory type the publisher sells through Equativ, whether a matching ads.txt or app-ads.txt line exists, whether its seller account ID matches the ID Equativ assigned, and whether the DIRECT or RESELLER designation reflects how the inventory is sold. A file that carries a correct Equativ line for every Equativ-sold inventory type passes. A missing line, a mismatched account ID, or a wrong relationship type is a fail recorded with the file and the specific field that is wrong.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Equativ lines from the live ads.txt and app-ads.txt files and the account details Equativ provides above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt file for {{site_name}} lacks a correct Equativ authorization, so the seller account ID or the DIRECT or RESELLER designation does not match how the inventory is sold through Equativ. A demand-side platform reads ads.txt to confirm a bid request is authentic, so a missing or wrong Equativ line can lead buyers to treat the inventory as unauthorized and decline to bid on impressions the publisher meant to sell. {{voice_recommend}} correcting the Equativ line so the advertising-system domain, the seller account ID, and the relationship type match the account details Equativ provides, and adding the matching line to app-ads.txt if app inventory is sold through Equativ. After the change, recheck the live file to confirm the corrected line is published and parses cleanly.

audit_checks.title: Stale, duplicate, or unauthorized Equativ lines remain in the file
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Lines left over from an ended Equativ relationship, a duplicate Equativ line, or a line authorizing a reseller path the publisher no longer uses keep authorizing supply that should no longer carry the publisher's name. That widens the set of sellers a buyer sees as legitimate beyond what the publisher actually sanctions, which weakens the protection ads.txt is meant to provide and can let inventory clear through a path the publisher meant to close.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is meant to be the current, accurate list of who may sell a publisher's inventory, so it carries weight only when it is kept current. Lines accumulate as a publisher adds sellers, and they do not remove themselves when a relationship ends. A line for an Equativ reseller path the publisher closed, a duplicate Equativ line added during a setup change, or an account ID for a seat that is no longer active all keep authorizing supply that the file should no longer sanction.

The purpose of the standard is to let buyers trust that a listed seller is genuinely authorized, which is the protection the IAB Tech Lab Authorized Digital Sellers project was built to provide. A file padded with stale or duplicate lines undercuts that purpose. It widens the set of sellers a buyer treats as legitimate beyond what the publisher actually uses, and it can keep a path open for inventory to clear through a relationship the publisher meant to end. Duplicate lines also make the file harder to audit since a reader cannot tell at a glance which Equativ line is the live one.

Removing a line is as consequential as adding one. A publisher that ends an Equativ relationship, or stops using a reseller path, may want to remove the corresponding line so the file stops authorizing that supply. The audit question is whether every Equativ line in the file maps to a relationship the publisher currently uses, with no leftovers and no duplicates.

A few signs point to stale, duplicate, or unauthorized lines:
  • More than one Equativ line shares the same advertising-system domain and account ID, which is a duplicate
  • An Equativ line names a seller account or reseller path the publisher no longer uses
  • The file carries RESELLER lines for partners the publisher cannot account for or did not knowingly authorize
  • The number of Equativ-related lines does not match the relationships the publisher can confirm are active
You may want to reconcile every Equativ line in the file against the relationships the publisher currently uses and remove any that no longer apply. One approach is to list each Equativ line, confirm with the publisher whether the relationship behind it is still active, and remove the lines that are not.

site_type_overlays.publishing: A media site that has changed monetization partners over the years carries the longest ads.txt history, so old Equativ or reseller lines can linger from setups that ended. You may want to reconcile the Equativ lines against the relationships the publisher can confirm today and remove the ones tied to partners no longer in use.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A commerce-content site that ran a limited monetization test through Equativ and then changed approach may still carry the lines from that test. You may want to confirm whether each Equativ line reflects a path the publisher actively sells through now, and remove any line left from a test or a relationship that has since ended.

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's record of the active seller account IDs and reseller paths for a publisher is in the partner portal (login required), which is the reference for confirming which lines are still current.

investigate.intro: List every Equativ-related line in the live ads.txt and app-ads.txt files, then reconcile each against the relationships the publisher currently uses to find duplicates and leftovers.

browser.label: Web browser
steps: Open the publisher's '/ads.txt' file and list every line whose first field is the Equativ advertising-system domain, with its account ID and relationship type.
Mark any two lines that share the same advertising-system domain and account ID as duplicates.
Ask the publisher to confirm, for each Equativ line, whether the relationship behind it is still active, and note the lines that are not.
Repeat the read on app-ads.txt if app inventory is sold through Equativ, and reconcile those lines the same way.

ads_txt_validator.label: Public ads.txt validator
steps: Run the publisher's domain through a public ads.txt validator and review the parsed list of seller lines.
Use the parsed output to spot duplicate Equativ lines and to count how many Equativ-related lines the file carries.
Compare that count against the number of active Equativ relationships the publisher can confirm, and record any excess as a candidate for removal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every Equativ-related line in the file and reconciles each against the relationships the publisher currently uses. A file where every Equativ line maps to an active relationship, with no duplicates and no leftovers, passes. A duplicate line, a line for a relationship the publisher no longer uses, or a line the publisher cannot account for is a fail recorded with the specific lines and the relationships they no longer match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Equativ lines from the live file and the relationships the publisher can confirm are active above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt file for {{site_name}} carries Equativ lines that no longer map to active relationships, whether duplicates or leftovers from a path the publisher has since closed. ads.txt protects a publisher only when it lists the sellers genuinely in use, so stale or duplicate lines widen the set of sellers buyers treat as authorized beyond what the publisher actually sanctions. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling every Equativ line against the relationships the publisher currently uses, removing the duplicates and the lines tied to ended relationships, and keeping only the lines that match an active seller path. After the cleanup, recheck the live file to confirm only the current Equativ lines remain.
